title: Thermal Shader Ball Coffee Mat Simulator
description: A physics-based thermal simulation where shader balls of varying temperatures are placed onto a coffee mat acting as a heat-conducting surface. Users drag shader balls onto the mat, adjust their initial temperatures, and observe heat transfer between balls through the mat medium, with total thermal energy conserved across the system. Undo/redo controls let users reverse placement and temperature changes.
prompt:
Create a single HTML file using Three.js r170 (importmap from cdn.jsdelivr.net) implementing a thermal conduction simulation. Load coffeemat.glb centered on the ground plane as a heat-conducting surface. Load ShaderBall2.glb as a template and allow the user to spawn up to 6 cloned shader balls by clicking a '+' button in an HTML overlay panel. Each ball appears at a staging area off the mat. Users can: (1) drag-and-drop balls onto specific grid snap positions on the mat (3x2 grid), (2) adjust each ball's temperature from 0-500K using a slider that appears when a ball is selected via raycasting click, (3) click 'Simulate' to run heat transfer where adjacent balls exchange energy through the mat following Newton's law of cooling (dQ = k*dt*(T1-T2)), with total system energy strictly conserved. Ball colors interpolate from blue (0K) to white (250K) to red (500K) using MeshStandardMaterial color and emissive properties updated each frame. The mat's color also subtly shifts based on average local temperature. Camera: OrbitControls, initial position (0, 4, 6) looking at origin. Lighting: one DirectionalLight with shadows enabled, one HemisphereLight for ambient fill. Interaction steps include spawning balls, dragging to grid slots, selecting balls, adjusting temperatures, running simulation, pausing, re-adjusting, and using undo/redo. Implement an undo/redo stack (Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y and on-screen buttons) that records every state change: ball spawn, ball position change, temperature slider change. Display a top-right HUD showing total system energy (sum of all temperatures as proxy), number of balls, and simulation timestep. The conservation law requires total energy before and after each simulation tick to remain constant within floating-point tolerance. Multi-input coupling means changing one ball's temperature during simulation redistributes heat to neighbors automatically. Add a 'Reset' button to clear all balls. Use requestAnimationFrame loop; simulation runs at fixed 60Hz substep when active.

[State Exposure (required)]
Expose window.__3D_STATE__ updated every animation frame:
window.__3D_STATE__ = {
  ballCount: number,         // current number of shader balls spawned (0-6)
  ballsOnMat: number,         // count of balls placed on mat grid positions
  simulationActive: boolean,         // whether thermal simulation is running
  totalSystemEnergy: number,         // sum of all ball thermal energies in joules
  averageMatTemperature: number,         // average temperature across all mat positions in Kelvin
  selectedBallIndex: number,         // index of currently selected ball (-1 if none)
  maxTemperatureBall: number,         // highest temperature among all balls in Kelvin
  minTemperatureBall: number,         // lowest temperature among all balls in Kelvin
  heatTransferRate: number,         // current rate of energy exchange in watts
  simulationTime: number,         // elapsed simulation time in seconds
  energyConservationError: number,         // deviation from initial total energy (should be near 0)
}
